learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, HashMap, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine you are shopping with a gift card worth exactly $100. Every product has a price. Instead of checking every pair of products manually, you remember every product price you've already seen. For each new product, you instantly calculate how much money is still needed and check whether you've already seen that price. This is exactly how the optimal HashMap solution works.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine you are shopping with a gift card worth exactly $100. Every product has a price. Instead of checking every pair of products manually, you remember every product price you've already seen. For each new product, you instantly calculate how much money is still needed and check whether you've already seen that price. This is exactly how the optimal HashMap solution works.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this approach whenever you need to find two values that satisfy a mathematical relationship (usually sum = target) in linear time. The HashMap complement pattern is one of the most common interview patterns.
learning.signals: Whenever a question asks for two numbers satisfying a mathematical relationship (sum, difference, complement), immediately think about storing previously seen values in a HashMap.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to the target. Assume that exactly one valid answer exists, and you may not use the same element twice. You may return the answer in any order.
constraints: Cannot use the same element twice.
edgeCases: Answer contains duplicate values at different indices

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Check every possible pair and return the pair whose sum equals the target.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of asking 'Which two numbers make the target?', think differently: 'For the current number, what number do I need to complete the target?' This required value is called the complement. If that complement has already appeared earlier, then we immediately found the answer.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a question asks for two numbers satisfying a mathematical relationship (sum, difference, complement), immediately think about storing previously seen values in a HashMap.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element is processed only once. HashMap provides constant average lookup and insertion. Therefore the entire solution runs in linear time.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a question asks for two numbers satisfying a mathematical relationship (sum, difference, complement), immediately think about storing previously seen values in a HashMap.
commonWrongPaths: Adding current number into HashMap before checking complement.
Sorting original array without preserving indices.

hints: Instead of searching for two numbers, search for one missing number.
Use a HashMap to achieve O(1) average lookup.
commonMisconceptions: Adding current number into HashMap before checking complement.
Sorting original array without preserving indices.

pattern.name: Hash Map Lookup
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a question asks for two numbers satisfying a mathematical relationship (sum, difference, complement), immediately think about storing previously seen values in a HashMap.

approaches.title: Nested Loop Approach
approaches.idea: Check every possible pair and return the pair whose sum equals the target.
algorithm: Loop j from i+1 to n-1.
If equal to target, return indices.

approaches.title: Sorting with Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Create value-index pairs, sort them, then use two pointers to locate the required sum.
algorithm: Move pointers based on current sum.
approaches.dryRun:
Pairs: [(2,0),(7,1),(11,2),(15,3)]
left=2 right=15 sum=17 >9
Move right
2+11=13
Move right
2+7=9 Found.

code.java: Sorting-based solution omitted for brevity. Uses Arrays.sort() with Pair objects.
code.python: Sorting-based solution using enumerate() and sorted().
code.javascript: Sorting-based solution using array of objects.

approaches.title: HashMap Complement Lookup
approaches.idea: Traverse the array once. For each number compute its complement (target - current). If the complement already exists in the HashMap, return the stored index and current index. Otherwise store the current number and index.
approaches.intuition: Each element is processed only once. HashMap provides constant average lookup and insertion. Therefore the entire solution runs in linear time.
algorithm: Otherwise insert current value with its index.

visualization.description: The HashMap stores numbers that have already been visited.

interviewFollowUps: Can you solve it without extra space?
What if the array is already sorted?
How would you solve Three Sum?
Can this work on a stream of incoming numbers?
interviewTips: Always explain brute force before optimization.
Explain why lookup occurs before insertion.

cheatSheet.algorithm: Lookup complement first. If found return. Else insert current.

commonMistakes: Adding current number into HashMap before checking complement.
Sorting original array without preserving indices.
